metadata.title: West Broadway/D Street Projects
metadata.author: Theo Fox
metadata.subtitle: The West Broadway Neighborhood Before Public Housing

caption.text: In 1949, the final brick was placed in the West Broadway Housing Development, offering homes to families of veterans returning from the war effort. Originally designated for white residents alone, this would be the first of four iterations of public housing in this South Boston neighborhood. The city would not desegregate the project until the 1960s, and while situated in the same neighborhood, the tenants of the West Broadway Homes today are very diverse.
caption.title: West Broadway Today

caption.text: The partial land clearing of this neighborhood, bordered by D Street, B Street, West Broadway, and West Seventh Street, would begin in 1941, with great focus placed on D street in particular...
caption.title: D Street

caption.text: ...largely due to the heavy concentration of dilapidated buildings seen in the first half of the twentieth century. The first iteration of the West Broadway Homes would be colloquially known as the "D Street Projects".
caption.title: D Street

caption.text: This 1960 map shows the perceptions of South Boston through the middle of the twentieth century, deeming neighborhoods as either "deteriorating", "deteriorated", or "renewal area". The conditions depicted on this map are a far cry from what city planners had envisioned when the land which would become Boston's South End was first filled.
caption.title: South End Renewal

caption.text: The tidal marshes of south of Boston's downtown would be filled in through the first half of the 19th century with the intention of providing homes for the city's wealthier residents. Yet growing industrial infrastructure, and the construction of the railroad depicted here, would drive property values down leading to a mass emigration of wealth. Wealth would be replaced with low rents and immigrant residents.
caption.title: The Beginnings of the South End

caption.text: Before the partial land clearance in 1941, this West Broadway neighborhood was home to Irish and eastern-European immigrants who relied on their Catholic faith to build a sense of community.
caption.title: Religious Commonalities

caption.text: The middle of the 19th century saw droves of Irish immigrants arriving in Boston, spurred by the devastation of the Great Hunger, and with city would push these arrivals into the South End. By 1886, the year this atlas page depicts, the West Broadway neighborhood was filled with Irish tenants...
caption.title: Early Arrivals

caption.text: ... many of whom would arrive at Constitution Wharf in the Boston Harbor.
caption.title: Early Arrivals

caption.text: This 1957 drawing depicts Irish immigrants rejoining with family who had arrived in the prior decades, yet a majority of these families were faced with hostility upon arrival to the predominately Protestant city, not only due to their Catholic faith...
caption.title: Early Perceptions

caption.text: ... but the typhus which many had contracted on over-crowded shifts which had brought them to Boston. City officials would keep meticulous records of those had contracted the disease. While the date of these records are unclear, J.B Upham would work with the city's health officials throughout the first the Irish immigrant typhus scare in the middle decades of the 19th century.
caption.title: Early Perceptions

caption.text: The city would build a quarantine hospital on Deer Island about eight miles from downtown Boston, shown on this 1882 atlas just off the tip of Point Shirley.
caption.title: Diseased Perceptions and Quarantine

caption.text: This image from 1847 depicts the women's quarter of the hospital. Hundreds would never leave Deer Island, and those who would still carried diseased perceptions in the eyes of the city's residents.
caption.title: Diseased Perceptions and Quarantine

caption.text: Those who survived arrival had little capital, and city officials would push them into the now lower-class neighborhoods of South Boston, this West Broadway neighborhood included.
caption.title: Forming Community

caption.text: Residents of this neighborhood would often work as unskilled laborers on the wharves just north of their new home...
caption.title: Finding Work

caption.text: ... often working as clam diggers, as shown here in this 1882 photograph, construction workers, and any other occupations deemed undesirable by the local population. Irish women would often find work as domestic servants.
caption.title: Finding Work

caption.text: The parish of Our Lady of the Holy Rosary would be consecrated in 1885 during a wave of efforts by the Diocese of Boston to support the city's growing population. It would become a pillar of the West Broadway neighborhood...
caption.title: Catholic Roots

caption.text: .. and this rectory was a part of the initial construction to provide housing for the parish priest.
caption.title: Catholic Roots

caption.text: It was smaller parish which experienced little growth over its fifty-six year existence, indicative of the lack of development the West Broadway neighborhood experienced before the housing developments.
caption.title: Forming Community

caption.text: The parish's founding pastor, John J. McNulty lived in the parish's rectory shown here, as would the church's consequent pastors who both lived in the neighborhood and served its spiritual and communal needs.
caption.title: Forming Community

caption.text: A majority of the residences in this four-block neighborhood were inhabited by families, evident through heavy concentration of schools.
caption.title: Forming Community

caption.text: The Norcross School, shown here on the corner of West Fifth and D Street, was opened in 1868 as an all-girls school...
caption.title: Communal Education

caption.text: ... and through the turn of the 20th century, its student body was representative of the neighborhood's dominant Irish Catholic Population.
caption.title: Communal Education

caption.text: Annie Duran lived at this location, 121 D Street. 1890s baptismal records show that she was baptized at Our Lady of the Holy Rosary in 1894, and as an Irish-Catholic resident of this neighborhood, she would have likely attended the Norcross School for Girls. Annie would marry Martin Mulkern at this same parish in 1919.
caption.title: The Residents

caption.text: Annie Duran's family name is not listed on the building in which she lived, and after cross-referencing the sacramental records of Our Lady of the Holy Rosary, none of the church's parishioners who lived in this neighborhood have their names listed on this 1919 atlas page. This is indicative of the working-class, borderline impoverished economic condition of the area... its residents were renters, not owners.
caption.title: The Residents

caption.text: The 20th century brought little material change to the buildings of this neighborhood, and Catholicism would continue to dominate its community. The Roman Catholic Church's Ne Temere decree in 1908 is noted here on the first page of the OLHR' marriage registry, establishing new rules for inter-denominational marriage.
caption.title: Marriage at Our Lady of the Holy Rosary

caption.text: The church would require dispensation letters for these occurrences, an example of which is seen here, written in 1929 by a Protestant man named Alfred.
caption.title: Marriage at Our Lady of the Holy Rosary

caption.text: Ellen T. Feeney, the Catholic woman Alfred was marrying lived at this location, 58 West 6th Street, yet it is unclear if the newlyweds remained in the neighborhood after marriage at OLHR.
caption.title: Marriage at Our Lady of the Holy Rosary

caption.text: On the same block as Ellen T. Feeney's pre-marital residence stands St. Peters Lithuanian Church. While the Irish were the first to settle in the West Broadway neighborhood, Catholic Lithuanian immigrants would begin to arrive at the end of the 19th century, spurred by political oppression under the Russian Empire. Just as OLHR had been built to serve the needs of the neighborhood's Irish community, this church was opened to support the growing Lithuanian population.
caption.title: Sharing the Neighborhood

caption.text: Facing Flaherty Way, this parish was consecrated in 1904, and is still an active pillar of Boston's Lithuanian community today.
caption.title: Sharing the Neighborhood

caption.text: In the decades before the 1941 land clearing, the multiethnic Catholic community had solidified the identity of this neighborhood, yet the lack of upward mobility faced by its immigrant population was reflected through the condition of the homes in which they lived. Residents who did manage to break through the constraints of poverty would love out of the neighborhood ...
caption.title: A "Blighted" Community (1924 photograph of Gold Street)

caption.text: ... leaving dilapidated homes behind.
caption.title: A "Blighted" Community (1940 photograph of West Seventh Street residence)

caption.text: Boston's rapidly growing population in the first half of the 20th century dramatically increased housing demands. City planners began using the terms "slum" and "blighted" to describe low-income residential neighborhoods in an attempt to justify land clearing. The West Broadway neighborhood, shown in this 1926 arial image of South Boston, would be put within the city's crosshairs.
caption.title: The Beginning of the End

caption.text: The official land clearing of the neighborhood was announced in the late 1930s, and active demolition commenced in 1941. The project would be stalled after the the U.S joined World War II, yet the stall would not save the neighborhood's wooden residential homes.
caption.title: The Beginning of the End

caption.text: This photograph of Gold Street was taken in 1940, likely the last capturing of these building. It would not only be residential homes demolished in the land clearance, but Our Lady of the Holy Rosary Parish as well.
caption.title: The Beginning of the End

caption.text: By chance, St. Peter Church would survive the clearance. When construction of the West Broadway Housing Development was completed in 1949, the church would be one of the few buildings which remained from the pre-development neighborhood. The photograph here depicts the housing development in the first decades after construction, with St. Peter Church now peering over rows of brick apartments.
caption.title: Lingering Memories
